text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

29D.1	The United Nations Office at Nairobi was established in 1996 as a successor to the United Nations Common Services Unit at Nairobi and the two separate divisions of administration of the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the United Nations Human Settlements Programme (UN-Habitat), to strengthen the United Nations presence in Nairobi and to achieve economies of scale, and provide management, information and communications technology (ICT) and administrative services to the United Nations complex in Nairobi. In its resolution 53/242, the General Assembly requested the Secretary-General to strengthen the Office and encouraged the Director-General of the United Nations Office at Nairobi to take steps to increase the level of utilization of the Office. Under a memorandum of understanding and specific service arrangements, the Office provides the Nairobi-based headquarters and globally dispersed offices of UNEP and UN-Habitat with a full range of administrative and other support services. As the United Nations Secretariat designated global and regional service provider in Nairobi, the Office provides human resources administration, finance services, international travel, recruitment of consultants and procurement services to the resident coordinator system and to Secretariat offices with a regional presence. In total, the Office provides services to United Nations entities in 166 countries. Under the framework of Kenya Common Back Office since 2024, the United Nations Office at Nairobi provides administrative, ICT, local procurement and local recruitment services to more than 90 offices of United Nations agencies, funds and programmes operating in or from Kenya and other United Nations system entities, including special political missions and peacekeeping support operations.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

29D.2	In recent years, multiple entities have relocated some of their functions and regional offices to the United Nations premises in Nairobi, including the United Nations Office for Project Services, the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights, the International Trade Centre, the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF), the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, the United Nations Population Fund and the United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women. In addition, the Office is expecting an increase in personnel from the International Civil Aviation Organization and UNICEF in 2026.

29D.3	In 2027, the Office will support its clients, including through the common back-office framework and the phase 1 of the common administrative platform in Nairobi, in support of the principles of the Pact for the Future and the efficiency agenda. The Office will increase the level of utilization of its services and continue to support the United Nations common premises, through partnerships with other United Nations entities, to maximize shared infrastructure and services and achieve efficiencies.

29D.4	For 2027, the Office will maintain optimal service delivery and continue to monitor key performance indicators through the use of analytical tools. The Office will further strengthen its financial management, internal control systems, risk framework and communication strategy, including through regular key performance indicator reporting, qualitative surveys and a client relations management platform.

29D.5	The major infrastructure project on the conference facilities remains high on the programme agenda, with the project main works continuing in 2027. Business continuity will remain a major focus in order to proceed with mandated calendar meetings, including the eighth session of the United Nations Environment Assembly and the fourth open-ended meeting of the Committee of Permanent Representatives, scheduled to take place in 2027. The project will continue to focus on engineering initiatives that provide the best value for money for Member States while delivering the project within the approved scope, budget and schedule.

29D.6	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, as the main interlocutor with the Government of Kenya, the Office will deepen its engagement with the host country Government in support of its mandate and the clients that it serves, representing all United Nations entities operating in or from Kenya.
29D.7	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	Nairobi-based organizations of the United Nations common system maintain a commitment to collaborate with the United Nations Secretariat on ongoing activities;

(b)	Extrabudgetary funding situation of client offices continues to allow for planning and implementation of the Office’s activities with adequate staffing levels, in particular for the expanded United Nations complex facilities, infrastructure and conference facility requirements.

29D.8	The Office integrates a gender perspective in its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. It supports the Secretary-General’s system-wide strategy on gender parity, provides guidance to clients on how to integrate gender parity throughout the recruitment process and keeps client entities informed of the impact of selection decisions. The Office also works to promote geographical representation and diversity, including by providing client entities with information on Member States that are unrepresented or underrepresented.

29D.9	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Office will ensure that all major repairs and replacements at the United Nations complex in Nairobi consider and address accessibility concerns. The Office will strengthen an inclusive organizational culture that is supportive of staff members with disabilities and staff who have dependants with disabilities, offering regular training opportunities on disability inclusion, organizing awareness-raising events and communicating available benefits and entitlements. These activities will be pursued with the active involvement of persons with disabilities and their representative organizations.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

29D.10	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. For example, in the light of the consolidation of payroll processing into a single global team, the Office will cease payroll operations, as reflected in the updated table of deliverables of subprogramme 1. The Joint Medical Services will cater more for the service requirements of the United Nations agencies, funds and programmes. Subprogramme 4 will refine its scope and focus on infrastructure, network and data, the provision of client services, the maintenance of conference centre technology and the provision of conference technology services.

29D.11	The Office will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals relating to the establishment of a common administrative platform in Nairobi, as part of the next phase of the consolidation and streamlining of support services and structures across the Secretariat.
Evaluation
29D.12	An evaluation of the common administrative platform for the United Nations Secretariat offices in Nairobi, to be conducted by the Office, is planned for 2027.
Legislative mandates
29D.13	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides all mandates entrusted to the programme.
General Assembly resolutions

children.text: Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Programme planning, finance and budget
Objective
29D.15	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the sound, effective and efficient financial management and reporting by client offices.
Strategy
29D.16	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Oversee the financial management and internal control of the regular budget and extrabudgetary funds of the programme and that of its clients;

(b)	Streamline the financial procedures and workflows of client offices and ensure strict adherence to the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations, including through assurance and visibility of United Nations resources management, monitored by improved financial dashboards for the programme and client entities;

(c)	Maintain the financial accounts of its clients, improve the financial integrity of data, prepare the financial statements in compliance with the International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS), support the internal and external audit processes, produce accurate and timely financial reports for donors and manage funding arrangements with implementing partners;

(d)	Support the Department of Operational Support in the implementation of payroll consolidation by providing the integrated support functions and common administrative platform services to support the successful delivery of integrated payroll services;
(e)	Support client offices on results-based management and to implement the enhanced delegation of authority framework;
(f)	Establish appropriate service rates and service requirements and monitor service statistics and client satisfaction;
(g)	Monitor and manage the financial risks of reform initiatives mandated to the United Nations Office at Nairobi.
29D.17	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Operational efficiency and improved stewardship of donor funds and other resources, through increased transparency and accountability, in accordance with the enhanced delegation of authority framework;
(b)	An unqualified audit opinion of the Board of Auditors for UNEP and UN-Habitat;
(c)	Enhanced visibility for Member States on the impact of their contributions to the programme and the project implementation of client entities.
Programme performance in 2025
Improved timeliness in processing accounting journals for clients

29D.18	The subprogramme enhanced the internal control integrated within the client relationship management system workflow, to reduce the processing time of the requests from clients for accounting journals. The workflow was enhanced to streamline the routing process of the requests through the appropriate levels of authorizations based on the segregation of duties, and to ensure complete documentation to fulfil audit requirements. These measures collectively ensured compliance with United Nations principles, IPSAS standards and delegation-of-authority requirements, providing robust assurance over the integrity of accounting adjustments. All enhancements were implemented in accordance with accounting guidelines and monitored against established key performance indicators. As a result, the subprogramme almost doubled the rate at which it processed accounting journals from 2023 to 2025, providing expedited services to its clients, while maintaining compliance with the internal control standards.
29D.19	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.I).
Figure 29D.I
Performance measure: percentage of accounting journals processed within established timelines (annual)
Note: The established timeline for processing accounting journals is 10 working days.
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: improved assets accounting for United Nations Office at Nairobi buildings, facilities and infrastructure – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
29D.20	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the recording of 99 per cent of capitalizable assets, which exceeded the planned target of 97 per cent.
29D.21	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.II).
Figure 29D.II
Performance measure: comprehensiveness of recording of capitalizable assets under construction in the financial statements (annual)
(Percentage of capitalizable assets recorded)
Result 2: improved timeliness in the resolution of rejected and returned payments – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29D.22	The subprogramme’s work contributed to an average of 41 days taken for the resolution of returned and rejected payment documents, which did not meet the planned target of 30 days. The target was not met due to a higher-than-anticipated volume of returned payments during the reporting period, primarily resulting from the change in the global payment platform that required updates of beneficiary banking details, changes in routing numbers and additional compliance and mandatory checks required by correspondent banks.
29D.23	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.III).
Figure 29D.III
Performance measure: average time taken for the resolution of rejected and returned payments (annual)
Result 3: improved timeliness for financial reporting to donors – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29D.24	Timely financial reporting to donors for their voluntary contributions is crucial to build trust, demonstrates accountability, facilitates continued support for the missions and the mandates of the United Nations entities and helps donors evaluate impact and make informed funding decisions. The subprogramme has been supporting clients in producing financial reports to donors. The subprogramme has also reduced reliance on the legacy system used prior to Umoja and produces the majority of its financial reports using Umoja. This is expected to significantly reduce the time for reconciliation and data completion, which in turn will facilitate the timeliness of the production of these reports.
Lessons learned and planned change

29D.25	The lesson for the subprogramme was the need to maintain close engagement and collaboration with the clients to ensure proactive monitoring of grants for which interim and final financial reports are due, and the importance of this collaboration in resolving incomplete accounting information. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will proactively monitor the due dates and potential issues that can delay issuance of financial reports to donors, provide its staff with up-to-date training to produce the reports, enhance the reporting functionalities of Umoja Analytics and engage closely with the clients to quickly resolve pending financial transactions.
29D.26	Expected progress is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.IV).
Figure 29D.IV
Performance measure: percentage of financial reports received by donors within established timelines (annual)
Note: The established timeline for preparing and issuing a donor report is 10 working days.
Deliverables
29D.27	Table 29D.2 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29D.2
Subprogramme 1: deliverables for the period 2025–2027, by category and subcategory

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Human resources management
Objective

29D.28	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the acquisition, development and retention of a competent, diverse, adaptable and healthy workforce with the highest standards of efficiency and integrity.
Strategy
29D.29	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Facilitate the recruitment process of personnel for its clients, ensuring fair and competitive recruitment processes;

(b)	Develop and refine tools to streamline the recruitment process and assist hiring managers in their evaluation of candidates, in collaboration with United Nations Headquarters and other offices away from Headquarters;

(c)	Foster greater interest from women and applicants from unrepresented and underrepresented countries, including through guidance to client entities to promote the selection of qualified candidates from these categories;

(d)	Develop and implement diverse training programmes for staff skills and competencies, in collaboration with other United Nations entities and external partners through jointly held training sessions;
(e)	Implement outreach programmes for all staff members, through briefings and intranet platforms, on benefits and entitlements and the related authorization processes;
(f)	Deliver a comprehensive health and well-being programme, including through promotion and awareness-raising on staff mental health and physical well-being, health assessments and advice.
29D.30	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased knowledge and skills of staff members;
(b)	Standardized services for clients across geographically dispersed locations;
(c)	Improved ability of client entities to respond to their shifting operational requirements;
(d)	Increased staff productivity.
Programme performance in 2025
Staff and personnel have access to enhanced well-being and mental health support

29D.31	The subprogramme increased efforts to engage clients in mental health and well-being awareness sessions, in addition to the routine health and counselling services offered by the Office’s Joint Medical Service. This was in response to an increase in demand for mental health and well-being services. Tailored sessions were offered on general health awareness and promotion of well-being, building mental health resilience, managing uncertainty and increasing capacity among staff to support each other and their families through peer support mechanisms.
29D.32	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.V).
Figure 29D.V
Performance measure: number of staff and personnel who participated in sessions on well-being and mental health (annual)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: staff and personnel acquire new knowledge and skills to improve organizational effectiveness – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29D.33	The subprogramme’s work contributed to a mandatory learning completion rate of 99 per cent, which exceeded the planned target of 90 per cent. The subprogramme’s work also contributed to the completion of instructor-facilitated classes by 3,349 personnel, which exceeded the planned target of 2,441; and the completion by 736 personnel of language programmes, which did not meet the planned target of 827. The target was not met due to the reprioritization of resources, which resulted in the discontinuation of a significant portion of language training.
29D.34	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.VI).
Figure 29D.VI
Performance measure: number of personnel completing instructor-facilitated classes and language programmes, and mandatory course compliance rate (annual)
Result 2: improved timeliness of staff benefits and entitlements services for all clients – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29D.35	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the accurate and timely delivery of staff benefits and entitlements services for the global service clientele of the Office, with 98 per cent of education grant requests, travel requests and other staff entitlements completed within established timelines, which exceeded the planned targets of 95 per cent, 92 per cent and 91 per cent, respectively.
29D.36	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.VII).
Figure 29D.VII
Performance measure: percentage of staff benefits and entitlements processes completed within established timelines
Note: The established timelines are 30 days for the education grant, 5 days for travel and 15 days for other staff entitlements.
Result 3: improved timeliness by client offices in recruitment pre-screening processes – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29D.37	Hiring personnel with the highest standards of efficiency, competence and integrity remains a key priority as the means to strengthen the Office’s programme implementation and its clients. Following the Secretariat-wide upgrade of the Inspira platform and issuance of administrative instructions on the staff selection system and the administration of appointments in 2025, the subprogramme will develop and support the capacity of hiring managers in the Office’s client entities by offering training and providing additional tracking tools.
Lessons learned and planned change

29D.38	The lesson for the subprogramme was that well-informed and trained hiring managers enable effective and efficient recruitment. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will train hiring managers on recruitment knowledge and skills, including on processes for pre-screening of candidates, and brief them on any changes to United Nations policies impacting recruitment. The subprogramme will continue to collaborate with hiring managers and provide them with tracking tools regarding the progress of recruitment cases. This will contribute to the effective and efficient execution of recruitment processes by hiring managers.
29D.39	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.VIII).
Figure 29D.VIII
Performance measure: percentage of human resources pre-screening assessments completed within five working days (annual)
Deliverables
29D.40	Table 29D.3 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29D.3
Subprogramme 2: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Administration: first-level review in administration of justice process; training and career development made available to 7,192 United Nations common system staff; performance management; inter-agency learning coordination of Nairobi-based entities; administration of 85 tests; 25 health campaigns; expert advice and guidance on staff selection processes and staff and non-staff hiring; annual staff survey on local salaries; administration of staff benefits and entitlements; outreach activities and guidance on human resources policies; administration of onboarding and separation from service; contract management; medical consultations; staff counselling services.

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Support services
Objective

29D.41	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the efficient, environmentally sound and effective functioning of client offices with regard to conference and facilities management, host country services, asset management, travel and transportation, mail and pouch services, commercial activities and procurement services, as well as archives and records management.
Strategy
29D.42	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide sustainable and efficient solutions with a specific focus on the management of energy, water, wastewater, waste and biodiversity, in accordance with International Organization for Standardization (ISO) 14001; and maintain the Office’s environmental management system, including the requisite ISO 14001-related post-certification annual internal audits and subsequent external annual surveillance activities, training and awareness;

(b)	Manage clients’ changing workspace needs by optimizing usage and working conditions in the available office space, including through redesign, in consultation with client entities, and through revisions of office space standards, to support the operational requirements of each tenant;
(c)	Provide staff transport solutions through an efficient distribution between internal and external transport service providers;
(d)	Provide immediate and effective travel, host country and property management services, especially leading up to major scheduled meetings;

(e)	Provide client-centric and solution-oriented procurement services to client entities and continuously strive to maximize value for money with due consideration to whole-life costs and effective international competition.
29D.43	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved physical infrastructure, optimized use of space and enhanced environmental management to support a sustainable, safe and flexible working environment;
(b)	Efficient and convenient travel, host country and property management services for all clients;
(c)	Efficient and convenient transport solutions in use by all clients;
(d)	Efficient and convenient procurement services available virtually to all global clients.
Programme performance in 2025
United Nations entities in Kenya have access to an events centre inside the United Nations complex in Nairobi

29D.44	The subprogramme, as a response to the service requirements for Kenya Common Back Office entities, has repurposed and introduced a more efficient use of an existing, previously underutilized building as a venue that features a diverse selection of indoor and outdoor settings, suitable for a broad array of small to medium-sized events for the entities hosted at the United Nations complex. Provision of this new events centre venue streamlines events management activities for entities by mitigating the costs, time, procurement and other logistical challenges associated with the use of external hotel venues elsewhere in Nairobi.
29D.45	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 29D.4).
Table 29D.4
Performance measure

table_content.text: 127 events were hosted within two indoor events spaces, two holding rooms, a covered terrace space for functions and an uncovered outdoor events venue space
157 events were hosted within two indoor events spaces, two holding/ bilateral spaces, a covered terrace space for functions and an additional two covered outdoor events venue spaces

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: new meeting and collaboration areas for delegates, staff and personnel – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
29D.46	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the availability of an additional 532 seats for meetings and collaboration for a total of 984 additional seats, which exceeded the planned target of 580.
29D.47	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.IX).
Figure 29D.IX
Performance measure: number of additional seats available for meeting and collaboration (cumulative)
Result 2: United Nations personnel in Kenya and the region receive smooth and efficient host country services – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
29D.48	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 2,847 personnel from 70 different United Nations entities receiving host country services in 2025, which exceeded the planned target of 2,755.
29D.49	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.X).
Figure 29D.X
Performance measure: number of United Nations personnel who receive efficient provision of host country services when onboarding in Kenya and the region (annual)
Result 3: improved conference service facilities that are integrated with the United Nations premises in Nairobi – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29D.50	The subprogramme supports the provision of conference services through the operations and maintenance of integrated facilities and associated infrastructure. In 2025, more than 6,000 delegates participated in the seventh United Nations Environment Assembly, far exceeding the capacity of the existing facilities. The subprogramme had to ensure the readiness of the conference facilities, increase the parking capacity in the complex and provide logistical support for the temporary spaces used to host the side events.
Lessons learned and planned change

29D.51	The lesson for the subprogramme was that servicing conferences require not only additional capacity and more flexible spaces with modern technology, but also integrated operations of the United Nations premises supporting the conference facilities. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will operate and manage the completed components of the conference facilities that will start to come online from 2027, as part of the Office’s complex facilities under the responsibilities of its central facilities management services. This will ensure seamless integration of conference service facilities with the entire United Nations complex facilities and infrastructure. This integration of services and facilities will meet the needs of the United Nations official calendar of meetings and the meetings of Member States and will provide adequate, safe and accessible conference and meeting spaces for delegates, participants, staff members and visitors, including the United Nations Environment Assembly and the UN-Habitat Assembly. The ultimate result will be improved conference facility services with an increase in capacity from the current 2,000 participants to 9,000 participants by 2029.
29D.52	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.XI).
Figure 29D.XI
Performance measure: capacity and size of conference and meeting spaces (cumulative)
Deliverables
29D.53	Table 29D.5 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29D.5
Subprogramme 3: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Administration: management and maintenance of United Nations-owned land/buildings (142 acres), including 17 office blocks and 4 logistics blocks, and 8 office blocks for the UNEP and UN-Habitat facilities, totalling 79 office floors and 14 conference rooms; management of office space and redesign of office layouts and configurations to serve the needs of the United Nations offices, other Secretariat departments and offices of other entities in Nairobi.

Logistics: receipt and inspection for 11 United Nations entities; issuance of 250 laissez-passer and 16,000 travel requests; travel arrangements for staff and meeting participants; handling of privileges and immunities for 71 United Nations entities; procurement, sourcing and issuance support of 5,000 lines of purchase orders; inventory advice, warehousing and disposal for 20 United Nations entities; shipment of official property, bulk consignments including for conferences, and the removal of personal effects; mail, pouch, distribution, registry and archiving services.

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Information and communications technology operations
Objective

29D.54	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the efficient and effective implementation of client entities’ substantive programmes through innovative technology solutions, while maintaining secure, coherent and resilient technology services and infrastructure backbone.
Strategy
29D.55	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Develop proposals for end-to-end software and hardware solutions in consultation with client entities and provide advice and technical support on the implementation of new technologies;
(b)	Support the roll-out of the Kenya Common Back Office and establish and maintain business relationships with clients to manage needs and requests;

(c)	Develop and enhance existing innovative processes to enable testing and adoption of new technologies and solutions while supporting the accessibility, reliability, consistency and sustainability of those solutions;

(d)	Collaborate closely with the Department for General Assembly and Conference Management in Nairobi, and with other subprogrammes to align and centralize under this subprogramme the end-to-end responsibilities of technical audiovisual services, engineering support and ICT infrastructure services for conference services at the Office;

(e)	Increase its focus on the infrastructure of the United Nations premises in Nairobi, including through support for the implementation of the conference facilities project; the upgrade and maintenance of conference technology; and provision of client services, in alignment with the information and communications technology strategy led by the Office of Information and Communications Technology.
29D.56	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Fulfilment of client entities’ business needs through the agile implementation of new and innovative technologies;
(b)	Empowerment of client entities to make effective decisions through access to more timely and responsive services;
(c)	Effective and efficient implementation of client entities’ substantive programmes.
Programme performance in 2025
A modernized information and communication technology platform that is compliant with United Nations Global Standards

29D.57 	The subprogramme implemented a phased modernization of its virtual server environment supporting critical United Nations applications. This initiative strengthened the reliability and resilience of ICT services, reduced long-term dependency on proprietary technologies and increased flexibility in hosting and scaling digital systems, which significantly improved the ability to deploy new systems quickly and shortened data recovery times without dependence on restrictive vendor licensing. The transition was carried out with minimal disruption to ongoing operations. The approach also enabled continued support for existing applications while facilitating the gradual adoption of more modern, scalable application designs, in full compliance with global United Nations ICT standards.
29D.58	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.XII).
Figure 29D.XII
Performance measure: percentage of core digital systems successfully transitioned into a modern and more flexible hosting environment
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: improved alignment of information and communications technology policies and practices in the United Nations – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29D.59	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 98 per cent of Secretariat entities adopting common back-office ICT service lines and to an 87 per cent adoption rate among non-Secretariat entities, which exceeded the planned target of 60 per cent.
29D.60	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.XIII).
Figure 29D.XIII
Performance measure: percentage of Nairobi-based United Nations Secretariat and non-Secretariat entities adopting all standard information and communications technology service lines (annual)
Result 2: smooth intergovernmental deliberations through the upgrade and maintenance of conference technology – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

29D.61	The subprogramme’s work contributed to a total of 3,505 meetings, including the seventh session of the United Nations Environmental Assembly, being successfully held without technological disruptions, through planned upgrades, which met the planned target.
29D.62	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see table 29D.6).

children.text: Result 3: enhanced network interoperability under a single service provider platform – Proposed programme plan for 2027

29D.63	The subprogramme provides campus-wide network connectivity, wireless access and network infrastructure services to support administrative operations and conference services, which until 2024 had been based on an operating model that was fixed and could not be tailored to the needs of individual clients.
Lessons learned and planned change

29D.64	The lesson for the subprogramme was that there was a need to transition its network infrastructure towards a service-provider operating model to better support a wide range of client requirements. This model also provides a clearer picture to all clients across the Gigiri campus regarding pricing and service expectations, while strengthening resilience and service continuity. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will migrate its network infrastructure towards a service-provider model, enabling clearer service definition, improved cost transparency and faster onboarding of clients. The subprogramme will further strengthen network resilience through expanded network monitoring capabilities and targeted improvements to the wireless network in high-demand areas. These measures aim to improve service reliability, reduce incident response times and ensure continuity of critical operations in support of business requirements.
29D.65	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 29D.XIV).
Figure 29D.XIV
Performance measure: percentage of entities transitioned to a service-provider model (cumulative)
Deliverables
29D.66	Table 29D.7 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 29D.7
Subprogramme 4: deliverables for 2027, by category and subcategory

table_content.text: Information and communications technology: technical support to over 4,600 end users; personal computing, voice, Internet, data and communication services for 3,663 users; information security compliance, software distribution and printing services for over 2,783 users; network connectivity services support to over 71 entities campus-wide through Wi-Fi and/or LAN (local area network) ports; cloud and data centre hosting services covering 274 physical server units and 111 virtual machines; preventive maintenance for 1,685 devices and equipment; operation of 22 application systems in a secure cloud environment; 11 outreach campaigns to clients.
